docs.title: Benchmarking flu surveillance tools with antigen-prime
docs.text: This repository contains the code and data needed to reproduce the analysis in our paper.

docs.title: Content
docs.text:
• Calculating Fitness
  • Variant Assignment
  • Growth Advantage Models

docs.title: Project layout
docs.text:
mkdocs.yml    # The configuration file.
docs/
    index.md  # The documentation homepage.
    ...       # Other markdown pages, images and other files.

docs.title: Calculating Fitness
docs.text: This page describes how we calculate fitness for tips (sampled viruses) based on their antigenic distance from host immune memory.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Fitness in this context represents the infection risk of a viral variant given the host population's immune memory. Variants that are antigenically distant from past infections have higher fitness (higher chance of infecting hosts).

docs.title: Single Timepoint Fitness Calculation
docs.text: Given a set of \(N\) tips with antigenic coordinates \((a_1^{(i)}, a_2^{(i)})\) and host immune memory coordinates \((h_1, h_2)\), the fitness \(f_i\) for tip \(i\) is:

docs.title: Step 2: Raw Risk
docs.text:
\[ r_i = d_i \cdot s \]

where \(s\) is the Smith conversion factor (default: \(s = 0.07\)).

docs.title: Step 3: Bounded Fitness
docs.text:
\[ f_i = \max\left(1 - \rho, \min(1, r_i)\right) \]

where \(\rho\) is homologous immunity (default: \(\rho = 0.95\)).

This bounds fitness to \([0.05, 1.0]\): tips identical to host memory have fitness \(0.05\), while maximally distant tips have fitness \(1.0\).

docs.title: Evaluating Variant Assignment Methods
docs.text: To assess how well a variant assignment method groups antigenically similar tips, we calculate the average within-variant fitness variance over time.

docs.title: Per-Timepoint Variance
docs.text:
For a variant assignment method \(M\) that partitions tips into \(K\) variants \(\{V_1, \ldots, V_K\}\):

  1. Compute fitness \(f_i\) for all tips using host coordinates at time \(t\)
  2. For each variant \(V_k\), calculate variance:
\[ \sigma^2_k = \text{Var}(\{f_i : i \in V_k\}) \]
  1. Average across variants:
\[ \bar{\sigma}^2_M(t) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sigma^2_k \]

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
• Low variance \(\Rightarrow\) tips within each variant have similar fitness \(\Rightarrow\) good antigenic clustering
  • High variance \(\Rightarrow\) tips within variants have heterogeneous fitness \(\Rightarrow\) poor antigenic clustering

This metric directly measures whether defined variants contain antigenically homogeneous populations.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Symbol Default Description Smith conversion \(s\) 0.07 Scales antigenic distance to infection risk Homologous immunity \(\rho\) 0.95 Immunity against identical antigens

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text: TODO: Create standalone script for batch processing across simulation runs.

docs.title: Empirical Growth Rates
docs.text: This page documents the calculation of empirical growth rates (\(r_{\text{data}}\)) for model evaluation.

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
The instantaneous growth rate for variant \(v\) at time \(t\):

\[ r_v(t) = \frac{\log I_v(t) - \log I_v(t - \Delta t)}{\Delta t} \]

where \(I_v(t)\) is variant incidence:

\[ I_v(t) = \pi_v(t) \cdot C(t) \]
  • \(\pi_v(t)\): variant frequency
  • \(C(t)\): total case count

docs.title: Calculation Pipeline
docs.text:
seq_counts.tsv + case_counts.tsv
    ↓ align to weekly boundaries
variant × time frequency matrix
    ↓ spline smoothing
smoothed frequencies + incidence
    ↓ log-difference
empirical growth rates (r_data)

docs.title: Step 1: Date Alignment
docs.text: Dates are aligned to weekly boundaries (Monday week starts) for consistent merging between sequence and case data.

docs.title: Step 2: Complete Frequency Matrix
docs.text: A complete variant × time matrix is constructed: - All variant-time combinations represented - Missing combinations filled with 0 - Frequencies calculated as \(\pi_v(t) = n_v(t) / N(t)\)

docs.title: Step 3: Spline Smoothing
docs.text: Raw counts and frequencies are smoothed to reduce noise.

docs.title: Spline Smoothing
docs.text:
Univariate splines minimize the penalized least squares objective:

\[ \hat{f}(t) = \text{argmin}_f \sum_i (y_i - f(t_i))^2 + s \int (f''(t))^2 dt \] Parameter Default Description s 1.0 Smoothing factor (larger = smoother) k 3 Spline degree (cubic)

docs.title: Post-Smoothing Processing
docs.text:
1. Clip smoothed frequencies to \([0, 1]\)
  2. Re-normalize frequencies to sum to 1.0 at each time point
  3. Calculate incidence: \(I_v(t) = \pi_v^{\text{smooth}}(t) \cdot C(t)\)

docs.title: Filtering Criteria
docs.text:
Points are filtered before computing growth rates:

Filter Default Rationale min_sequence_count 10 Low counts → unstable frequencies min_variant_frequency 0.01 Rare variants → noisy growth rates min_variant_incidence 50 Low incidence → unreliable estimates min_segment_length 3 Short segments → insufficient data skip_first_n_points 2 Initial points often noisy

docs.title: Segment Filtering
docs.text: Growth rates are only trusted for contiguous segments of valid data. The connect_gaps option controls whether gaps in data are bridged or treated as segment breaks.

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
The pipeline is implemented in antigentools/analysis.py:

from antigentools.analysis import get_filtered_growth_rates_df

growth_rates_df = get_filtered_growth_rates_df(
    build="flu-simulated-150k-samples",
    model="GARW",
    location="tropics",
    pivot_date="2040-10-01",
    spline_smoothing_factor=1.0,
    spline_order=3,
    min_sequence_count=10,
    min_variant_frequency=0.01,
    min_variant_incidence=50.0
)

docs.title: Output Columns
docs.text: Column Description variant Variant identifier date Observation date sequences Raw sequence count smoothed_sequences Spline-smoothed count variant_frequency Raw frequency variant_frequency_smoothed Smoothed + renormalized frequency cases Total case count variant_incidence_smoothed \(\pi_v^{\text{smooth}} \cdot C\) growth_rate_r_data Empirical growth rate median_r Model-predicted growth rate abs_error $

docs.title: Window-Level
docs.text:
Aggregate performance per analysis window (pivot date + location):

Metric Formula Correlation \(\rho(r_{\text{model}}, r_{\text{data}})\) MAE $\frac{1}{n}\sum RMSE \(\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum (r_{\text{model}} - r_{\text{data}})^2}\) \(R^2\) \(1 - SS_{\text{res}} / SS_{\text{tot}}\)

docs.title: Variant-Level
docs.text:
Per-variant metrics within each window:

from antigentools.analysis import calculate_variant_mae

variant_metrics = calculate_variant_mae(
    growth_rates_df,
    min_sequence_count=10,
    min_variant_frequency=0.01,
    min_variant_incidence=50.0
)

Additional variant-level outputs: - normalized_mae: MAE / max(\(|r_{\text{data}}|\)) - total_sequences: Sum of sequences for variant - max_variant_frequency: Peak frequency achieved

docs.title: Scoring Script
docs.text:
Batch evaluation across all windows:

python scripts/score_growth_rates.py \
    --config configs/benchmark_config.yaml \
    --build flu-simulated-150k-samples \
    --output-dir results/flu-simulated-150k-samples/

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text: File Description growth_rate_scores.tsv Window-level metrics variant_growth_rates.tsv Variant-level metrics vi_convergence_diagnostics.tsv Inference diagnostics growth-rates/{MODEL}/growth_rates_{loc}_{date}.tsv Per-window growth rates

docs.title: Growth Advantage Models
docs.text: This page documents the variant frequency forecasting models implemented in scripts/run_model.py.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Model Growth Rate Requires Cases Key Feature MLR Fixed per variant No Linear trend in logit space FGA Fixed per variant Yes Renewal model with fixed growth advantage GARW Time-varying Yes Random walk on growth advantages

docs.title: Model 1: Multinomial Logistic Regression (MLR)
docs.text: The simplest model — fits a linear trend in logit-frequency space for each variant.

docs.title: Model Structure
docs.text:
Variant frequencies \(\pi_v(t)\) are modeled via softmax over linear predictors:

\[ \pi_v(t) = \frac{\exp(\alpha_v + \beta_v t)}{\sum_{v'} \exp(\alpha_{v'} + \beta_{v'} t)} \]

where: - \(\alpha_v\) is the intercept for variant \(v\) - \(\beta_v\) is the growth rate (slope) for variant \(v\) - \(t\) is time in days

docs.title: Likelihood
docs.text:
Sequence counts follow a multinomial distribution:

\[ \mathbf{n}(t) \sim \text{Multinomial}(N(t), \boldsymbol{\pi}(t)) \]

where \(N(t)\) is total sequences at time \(t\).

docs.title: Effective Reproduction Number
docs.text:
\(R_t\) is derived from the growth rate using a fixed generation time \(\tau\):

\[ R_v = \exp(\beta_v \cdot \tau) \]

Default: \(\tau = 3.0\) days.

docs.title: Forecasting
docs.text:
Future frequencies are extrapolated by extending the linear predictor:

\[ \hat{\pi}_v(t + \Delta) = \text{softmax}(\alpha_v + \beta_v(t + \Delta)) \]

docs.title: Model 2: Fixed Growth Advantage (FGA)
docs.text: A renewal model where each variant has a fixed growth advantage relative to a baseline.

docs.title: Model Structure
docs.text:
The effective reproduction number for variant \(v\) at time \(t\) is:

\[ R_v(t) = R_{\text{base}}(t) \cdot \exp(\delta_v) \]

where: - \(R_{\text{base}}(t)\) is the baseline reproduction number (modeled with spline basis functions) - \(\delta_v\) is the fixed growth advantage for variant \(v\)

docs.title: Priors
docs.text:
\[ \delta_v \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_\delta^2) \]

Default: \(\sigma_\delta = 0.1\)

docs.title: Renewal Equation
docs.text:
Expected incidence for variant \(v\) follows the discrete renewal equation:

\[ I_v(t) = R_v(t) \sum_{s=1}^{S} g(s) \cdot I_v(t-s) \]

where \(g(s)\) is the discretized generation interval distribution.

docs.title: Generation Interval
docs.text:
The generation interval is modeled as a discretized Gamma distribution:

\[ g \sim \text{Gamma}(\mu_g, \sigma_g) \]

Default: \(\mu_g = 3.0\) days, \(\sigma_g = 1.2\) days

docs.title: Likelihoods
docs.text:
Case counts — Zero-inflated Negative Binomial:

\[ C(t) \sim \text{ZI-NegBinom}(\mu = \sum_v I_v(t) * d, \phi) \]

where \(d\) is the reporting delay distribution and \(\phi\) is the concentration parameter.

Sequence counts — Dirichlet-Multinomial:

\[ \mathbf{n}(t) \sim \text{DirMult}(N(t), \kappa \cdot \boldsymbol{\pi}(t)) \]

where \(\kappa\) controls overdispersion (default: 100).

docs.title: Model 3: Growth Advantage Random Walk (GARW)
docs.text: Extends FGA by allowing growth advantages to vary over time via a random walk.

docs.title: Model Structure
docs.text:
\[ R_v(t) = R_{\text{base}}(t) \cdot \exp(\delta_v(t)) \]

where \(\delta_v(t)\) evolves as a random walk:

\[ \delta_v(t) = \delta_v(t-1) + \epsilon_v(t), \quad \epsilon_v(t) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_\epsilon^2) \]

docs.title: Priors
docs.text:
Initial growth advantage: $$ \delta_v(0) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_\delta, \sigma_\delta^2) $$

Default: \(\mu_\delta = 0.1\), \(\sigma_\delta = 0.01\)

docs.title: Key Difference from FGA
docs.text:
• FGA: Single \(\delta_v\) per variant (time-invariant)
  • GARW: \(\delta_v(t)\) trajectory per variant (time-varying)

This allows GARW to capture changing fitness landscapes, but requires more data to estimate reliably.

docs.title: Basis Functions
docs.text:
\(R_{\text{base}}(t)\) is parameterized using B-spline basis functions:

\[ \log R_{\text{base}}(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{K} c_j B_j(t) \]

Default: order 4 splines with \(K = 10\) knots.

docs.title: Model Configuration
docs.text: This page describes inference settings and configuration for growth advantage models.

docs.title: Inference
docs.text: All models use Stochastic Variational Inference (SVI) via the evofr package.

docs.title: Default Settings
docs.text: Parameter Value Description iters 50,000 Optimization iterations lr 0.01 Learning rate num_samples 500 Posterior samples to draw

docs.title: Configuration Files
docs.text:
Model parameters can be customized via JSON config files:

python scripts/run_model.py \
    --data_path data/build/2040-10-01/ \
    --country tropics \
    --model GARW \
    --output_dir results/ \
    --config configs/default_config.json

docs.title: Schema Overview
docs.text:
{
  "seed_L": 14,
  "forecast_L": 365,
  "inference": {
    "method": "InferFullRank",
    "settings": {
      "iters": 50000,
      "lr": 0.01,
      "num_samples": 500
    }
  },
  "generation_time": {
    "distribution": "gamma",
    "parameters": {"mean": 3.0, "std": 1.2}
  },
  "model_specific": {
    "MLR": {"tau": 3.0},
    "FGA": {...},
    "GARW": {...}
  }
}

docs.title: Key Sections
docs.text: Section Purpose seed_L Days to seed renewal models forecast_L Forecast horizon in days inference SVI settings generation_time Generation interval distribution delay_distribution Reporting delay distribution model_specific Per-model hyperparameters

docs.title: MLR
docs.text:
"MLR": {
  "tau": 3.0
}
Parameter Default Description tau 3.0 Generation time for \(R_t\) calculation

docs.title: FGA
docs.text:
"FGA": {
  "ga_prior": 0.1,
  "case_likelihood": {
    "type": "ZINegBinomCases",
    "concentration": 0.05
  },
  "seq_likelihood": {
    "type": "DirMultinomialSeq",
    "concentration": 100
  }
}
Parameter Default Description ga_prior 0.1 Prior std on growth advantages (\(\sigma_\delta\)) case_likelihood.concentration 0.05 NegBinom concentration seq_likelihood.concentration 100 Dirichlet concentration (\(\kappa\))

docs.title: GARW
docs.text:
"GARW": {
  "ga_prior_mean": 0.1,
  "ga_prior_std": 0.01,
  "prior_family": "Normal",
  "case_likelihood": {...},
  "seq_likelihood": {...}
}
Parameter Default Description ga_prior_mean 0.1 Prior mean on initial \(\delta_v(0)\) ga_prior_std 0.01 Random walk innovation std (\(\sigma_\epsilon\)) prior_family "Normal" Prior distribution family

docs.title: Generation Time
docs.text:
Time between successive infections in a transmission chain.

"generation_time": {
  "distribution": "gamma",
  "parameters": {
    "mean": 3.0,
    "std": 1.2
  }
}

Used for: - MLR: Converting \(\beta_v\) to \(R_v\) - FGA/GARW: Renewal equation convolution kernel

docs.title: Delay Distribution
docs.text:
Reporting delay from infection to case observation.

"delay_distribution": {
  "distribution": "lognorm",
  "parameters": {
    "mean": 3.1,
    "std": 1.0
  }
}

docs.title: Convergence Diagnostics
docs.text:
VI convergence is tracked via ELBO trajectory. Diagnostics are saved to:

results/{build}/convergence-diagnostics/{MODEL}_{location}_{date}_vi_diagnostics.json

Key metrics: - initial_loss / final_loss: ELBO values - relative_change: Fractional improvement in final window - converged: Boolean based on threshold (default: 0.5)

docs.title: Running Simulations
docs.text: scripts/run_all_simulations.py is the batch entry point for processing all simulations under a given experiment / parameter-set directory. It discovers simulation directories, skips already-complete runs, then either executes run_pipeline.py locally (with optional parallelism) or writes SLURM array-job artifacts and exits.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
python scripts/run_all_simulations.py \
    --experiments-root PATH \
    --experiment EXP \
    --param-set PARAM_SET \
    --batch-name BATCH \
    --config configs/pipeline_config.yaml \
    --mode [local | slurm] \
    [--max-parallel N] \
    [--slurm-config configs/slurm_config.yaml]

docs.title: Required Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description --experiments-root Root of the antigen-experiments/experiments/ directory --experiment Experiment directory name (e.g. 2026-01-06-mutation-bug-fix-runs) --param-set Parameter-set subdirectory name --batch-name Human-supplied batch name; namespaces outputs under results/ --config Path to pipeline_config.yaml --mode local or slurm

docs.title: Optional Arguments
docs.text: Argument Default Description --max-parallel 1 Max concurrent subprocesses in local mode --slurm-config None Path to slurm_config.yaml; uses built-in defaults if omitted

docs.title: Local mode
docs.text:
Runs run_pipeline.py for each incomplete simulation as a subprocess.

  • --max-parallel 1 (default): serial execution, equivalent to a manual loop.
  • --max-parallel N (N > 1): uses ProcessPoolExecutor; automatically passes --set-thread-caps to each subprocess to prevent BLAS oversubscription (OMP_NUM_THREADS, MKL_NUM_THREADS, OPENBLAS_NUM_THREADS, JAX_PLATFORMS=cpu are set inside each worker).
# Serial
python scripts/run_all_simulations.py \
    --experiments-root /data/antigen-experiments/experiments \
    --experiment 2026-01-06-mutation-bug-fix-runs \
    --param-set nonEpitopeAcceptance_0.1_epitopeAcceptance_1.0 \
    --batch-name my-batch \
    --config configs/pipeline_config.yaml \
    --mode local

# Parallel (4 workers)
python scripts/run_all_simulations.py \
    ... \
    --mode local \
    --max-parallel 4

docs.title: SLURM mode
docs.text:
Writes sim_list.txt and submit_array.sh under results/<batch-name>/slurm_submission_<timestamp>/ then exits without calling sbatch.

python scripts/run_all_simulations.py \
    --experiments-root /data/antigen-experiments/experiments \
    --experiment 2026-01-06-mutation-bug-fix-runs \
    --param-set nonEpitopeAcceptance_0.1_epitopeAcceptance_1.0 \
    --batch-name my-batch \
    --config configs/pipeline_config.yaml \
    --mode slurm \
    --slurm-config configs/slurm_config.yaml

# Then submit manually:
sbatch results/my-batch/slurm_submission_<timestamp>/submit_array.sh

docs.title: Simulation Discovery
docs.text:
Globs <experiments-root>/<experiment>/<param-set>/run_*/ for directories and validates each contains both required input files before enqueuing:

  • output/run-out.tips
  • out_timeseries.csv

Discovery never reads from data/ or results/.

docs.title: Skip Logic
docs.text:
A simulation is considered complete — and skipped — only when both sentinel files exist under results/<batch-name>/<param-set>__<run-id>/:

  • scores.tsv
  • growth_rate_scores.tsv

One skipped-simulation log line is printed per skipped sim. Resubmitting after partial completion produces a shorter sim_list.txt covering only the remaining sims.

docs.title: SLURM Configuration
docs.text:
configs/slurm_config.yaml controls the generated SLURM script. Edit it before submitting on a new cluster.

slurm:
  partition: campus-new
  time: "08:00:00"
  mem_gb: 32
  cpus_per_task: 4
  max_concurrent: 20
  conda_env: antigen
  log_dir: logs/slurm/  # relative to project_root
  project_root: /fh/fast/matsen_e/<yourname>/antigen-forecasting  # update before submitting
  python_bin: python  # override if conda activate does not put python on PATH
Key Description partition SLURM partition name time Wall-clock time limit per task mem_gb Memory per task in GB cpus_per_task CPUs per array task max_concurrent Max simultaneously running array tasks conda_env Conda environment to activate log_dir Directory for .out/.err logs (relative to project_root) project_root Absolute path to the repo on the cluster python_bin Python executable to use

If --slurm-config is omitted, built-in defaults are used (repo-local paths — almost certainly wrong on the cluster; always pass the config for cluster runs).

docs.title: Local mode
docs.text:
Per-simulation outputs land in the standard locations written by run_pipeline.py:

data/<build>/
results/<batch-name>/<param-set>__<run-id>/scores.tsv
results/<batch-name>/<param-set>__<run-id>/growth_rate_scores.tsv

docs.title: SLURM mode
docs.text:
results/<batch-name>/slurm_submission_<timestamp>/
    sim_list.txt        # one sim path per line (1-indexed, matches array task IDs)
    submit_array.sh     # executable SLURM array script

aggregate.tsv is written separately (Issue 5) and never by this script.

docs.title: Variant Assignment
docs.text: This page describes three methods for assigning variant labels to sampled viral tips. Each method groups tips based on different biological features, enabling comparison of how well different data types capture antigenic similarity.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Method Input Clustering Key Assumption Antigenic ag1, ag2 coordinates k-means Antigenic space directly reflects immune escape Sequence nucleotide sequences k-means on embeddings Sequence similarity correlates with antigenic similarity Phylogenetic phylogenetic tree clade suggestion algorithm Tree topology + epitope mutations define variants

docs.title: Method 1: Antigenic Clustering
docs.text: Simplest approach — directly clusters tips in antigenic coordinate space.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Extract antigenic coordinates \((a_1, a_2)\) for each tip
  2. Apply k-means clustering with \(k\) clusters
  3. Assign variant labels based on cluster membership

docs.title: Inputs
docs.text: • Tips DataFrame with ag1, ag2 columns

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans

def assign_antigenic_variants(tips_df, n_clusters=30, random_state=42):
    coords = tips_df[['ag1', 'ag2']].values
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=n_clusters, random_state=random_state)
    tips_df['variant_ag'] = kmeans.fit_predict(coords)
    return tips_df

docs.title: Method 2: Sequence-Based Clustering
docs.text: Uses sequence embeddings to capture genetic relationships, then clusters in embedding space.

docs.title: Pipeline
docs.text:
sequences.fasta
    ↓ augur align
alignment.fasta
    ↓ pathogen-distance
distance_matrix.csv
    ↓ pathogen-embed (t-SNE)
embeddings.csv
    ↓ k-means
variant_labels

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Align sequences against reference using augur align
  2. Compute pairwise distances using pathogen-distance
  3. Learn embeddings using pathogen-embed (t-SNE or other method)
  4. Cluster embeddings using k-means

docs.title: Inputs
docs.text:
• sequences.fasta — nucleotide sequences for each tip
  • ref_HA.fasta — reference sequence for alignment

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
bash scripts/make_sequence_embeddings.sh

Key parameters in script:

EMBEDDING_METHOD="tsne"
N_COMPONENTS=10
N_CLUSTERS=30

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
• embeddings.csv — low-dimensional representation of sequences
  • seq-clusters.csv — variant assignments

docs.title: Method 3: Phylogenetic Clade Assignment
docs.text: Uses tree topology and mutation information to suggest clade boundaries. Unlike k-means methods, this produces hierarchical variant labels.

docs.title: Algorithm Overview
docs.text:
The clade suggestion algorithm (add_new_clades.py) assigns clades based on:

  1. Bushiness score — measures downstream tip diversity (similar to LBI)
  2. Branch score — weighted count of mutations at epitope sites
  3. Divergence score — nucleotide divergence since last clade breakpoint

A new clade is suggested when:

\[ \text{score} + \text{div\_score} > \text{cutoff} \]

where:

\[ \text{score} = \frac{\text{bushiness}}{\text{bushiness} + \text{scale}} + \frac{\text{mut_weight}}{\text{branch_scale} + \text{mut_weight}} \]

docs.title: Pipeline
docs.text:
sequences.fasta
    ↓ augur align
alignment.fasta
    ↓ augur tree (IQ-TREE)
tree_raw.nwk
    ↓ augur refine
tree.nwk
    ↓ augur ancestral
nt_muts.json
    ↓ augur translate
aa_muts.json
    ↓ augur export v2
auspice_base.json
    ↓ add_new_clades.py
auspice.json (with clade labels)

docs.title: Inputs
docs.text:
• sequences.fasta — nucleotide sequences
  • metadata.tsv — tip metadata (strain, date, etc.)
  • ref_HA.gb — reference GenBank file with gene annotations
  • weights.json — per-site mutation weights (epitope sites weighted higher)

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
bash scripts/assign-tree-based-variants.sh \
    --sequences data/build/sequences.fasta \
    --output-dir data/build/phylo-variants/ \
    --metadata data/build/metadata.tsv

docs.title: Key Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Default Description cutoff 1.0 Score threshold for new clade min_size 25 Minimum tips for valid clade bushiness_branch_scale 1.0 Controls phylo score sensitivity divergence_scale 2.0 Controls divergence score sensitivity branch_length_scale 2.0 Controls mutation weight sensitivity

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
• auspice.json — tree with clade annotations viewable in Auspice
  • Clade labels accessible via node_attrs['clade']

docs.title: Comparing Methods
docs.text:
See Calculating Fitness for how we evaluate variant assignment quality using within-variant fitness variance.

Key insight: A good variant assignment method should group tips with similar antigenic properties, resulting in low within-variant fitness variance.
